objects.claim: Propiconazole (100 µM) decreases cell viability in differentiated human SH-SY5Y neuroblastoma cells after 24 and 48 hours of exposure.

study_context.tissue: SH-SY5Y differentiated neuroblastoma cells (dopaminergic surrogate, in vitro PD model)

source.quoted_span: Cell viability was decreased with 100 μM propiconazole after 24 and 48 h, while tebuconazole required higher doses to affect viability (−200 μM at 24 h).

limitations: Supra-physiological micromolar doses; relevance to human occupational/dietary exposure unestablished
SH-SY5Y is a cancer-derived line, not primary dopaminergic neurons
No in vivo corroboration from the same paper

objects.claim: Propiconazole (50 µM) reduces mitochondrial membrane potential (MMP) in differentiated SH-SY5Y cells after 24 hours, at a lower dose than tebuconazole (200 µM required).

source.quoted_span: Mitochondrial membrane potential (MMP) was reduced with 50 μM propiconazole after 24 h while 200 μM tebuconazole reduced MMP.

limitations: Mitochondrial membrane depolarisation measured by single fluorescent dye; not confirmed by respirometry at this dose tier
Mechanism of MMP collapse (direct electron transport chain inhibition vs. secondary lipid disruption) not resolved in this study
Supra-physiological doses

objects.claim: Propiconazole impairs mitochondrial bioenergetics by decreasing basal oxygen consumption rate (basal respiration) in differentiated SH-SY5Y cells, measured by Seahorse XFe24 flux analysis at 100 µM.

study_context.route: In vitro treatment, Seahorse XFe24 Flux Analyzer

source.quoted_span: Propiconazole affects mitochondrial bioenergetics by decreasing basal respiration.

limitations: Specific electron transport chain complex(es) inhibited not determined in this study
Dose is pharmacologically high; environmental relevance unclear
Single in vitro system; no confirmation in primary neurons or in vivo models

objects.claim: Indiscriminate overuse or occupational exposure to triazole agricultural chemicals, including propiconazole, can lead to neurotoxicity via mitochondrial impairment that may exacerbate neurodegeneration.

study_context.species: Human (epidemiological inference from in vitro data)

source.quoted_span: Indiscriminate overuse or occupational exposure to agricultural chemicals can lead to neurotoxicity. Many pesticides act to impair mitochondrial function which can lead to exacerbation of neurodegeneration.

limitations: This is a framing statement by the authors, not a direct epidemiological finding
No human cohort data for propiconazole and PD risk in this paper
Extrapolation from high-dose in vitro data to real-world exposures is unvalidated

objects.claim: Propiconazole and tebuconazole effects on reactive oxygen species (ROS) production were measured in differentiated SH-SY5Y cells, establishing that oxidative stress is an endpoint of triazole fungicide neurotoxicity in a PD-relevant cellular model.

pathway_edge.target_node: Reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation in neuronal cells

study_context.dose: Not specified for ROS tier in available abstract text

source.quoted_span: Cell viability (based on ATP levels), mitochondrial membrane potential, oxidative respiration, and reactive oxygen species (ROS) were measured following fungicide treatments.

limitations: Direction and magnitude of ROS change by propiconazole specifically not extractable from available abstract text
ROS measurement methodology (dye, timepoint, dose) not specified in excerpt
Without full-text access, dose-response for ROS cannot be confirmed

objects.claim: Specific ceramides and fatty acids altered by propiconazole in SH-SY5Y cells are associated with mitochondrial dysfunction and neurotoxicity, providing a lipidomic mechanistic link between triazole exposure and PD-relevant neurodegeneration.

source.quoted_span: Some ceramides and fatty acids are associated with mitochondrial dysfunction and neurotoxicity.

limitations: Association between ceramide/fatty acid changes and neurotoxicity is correlative, not proven causal in this study
Specific ceramide species and their PD-relevance not enumerable from abstract text alone
Lipidomic signatures may reflect general cytotoxicity rather than PD-pathway-specific disruption
No functional rescue experiments linking lipid changes to neuronal death

objects.claim: Propiconazole is more potent as a neurotoxin to SH-SY5Y cells than the structurally related triazole fungicide tebuconazole across the endpoints of cell viability, MMP, and oxidative respiration.

source.quoted_span: Propiconazole is more neurotoxic to SH-SY5Y cells than tebuconazole based on the endpoints measured.

limitations: Comparative potency between two triazoles does not establish absolute risk
Chemical-structural basis of differential potency (e.g., lipophilicity, CYP14DM binding affinity) not elucidated
No dose-response modelling for benchmark dose derivation

objects.claim: The triazole fungicide neurotoxicity study in SH-SY5Y cells was explicitly designed to provide mechanistic insight into pesticide toxicity relevant to Parkinson's disease, using lipidomics to link ceramide and fatty acid alterations to mitochondrial dysfunction.

source.quoted_span: This study provides insight into pesticide toxicity and Parkinson's disease using lipidomics.

limitations: Lipidomics findings are associative; causal link between propiconazole-altered lipids and PD-relevant neurodegeneration not demonstrated
Ceramide/fatty acid changes may reflect general cytotoxicity rather than PD-specific pathology
Authors' framing is suggestive, not a confirmed mechanistic connection to PD

objects.claim: Triazole fungicides, including propiconazole, inhibit cytochrome P450-dependent 14α-sterol demethylase (CYP51), leading to depletion of ergosterol and disruption of membrane fluidity and function, which is the canonical primary mechanism of action of this fungicide class.

source.quoted_span: Specifically, triazoles act to inhibit cytochrome P-450-dependent 14α- sterol demethylase, which leads to depletion of ergosterol, a predominant part of the cell membrane that maintains its fluidity and function.

limitations: Ergosterol depletion is fungal-specific; the mammalian neuronal analogue (cholesterol metabolism disruption via CYP51 or related mammalian CYPs) is mechanistically distinct and not directly demonstrated for propiconazole in neurons in this study
Bridge from CYP51 inhibition to mitochondrial dysfunction in mammalian neurons is inferred, not directly established here

objects.claim: Inhibition of mitochondrial respiratory chain complex I by environmental pesticides leads to degeneration of dopaminergic neurons; an Adverse Outcome Pathway (AOP:3) formalises this as: complex I inhibition → mitochondrial dysfunction → dopaminergic neuron death → parkinsonian motor deficits.

chemical_identity.name: Propiconazole
chemical_identity.form: parent compound, triazole fungicide (complex I inhibition not directly confirmed for propiconazole; evidence is grounding via AOP:3)

study_context.species: Human and rodent (AOP based on multiple species data)
study_context.dose: Variable (AOP is dose-agnostic at the pathway level)

source.quoted_span: Inhibition of complex I of the mitochondrial respiratory chain (cI) by rotenone and methyl-phenylpyridinium (MPP+) leads to the degeneration of dopaminergic neurons in man and rodents. To formally describe this mechanism of toxicity, an adverse outcome pathway (AOP:3) has been developed that implies that any inhibitor of cI, or possibly of other parts of the respiratory chain, would have the potential to trigger parkinsonian motor deficits.

limitations: Propiconazole was not one of the 21 pesticides tested in this specific study; AOP:3 applicability to propiconazole is inferred from its mitochondrial bioenergetics effects in SH-SY5Y cells (DOI: 10.1016/j.neuro.2020.06.009)
Whether propiconazole specifically inhibits complex I vs. other respiratory chain components is not directly established
AOP:3 is a framework; stressor-specific evidence for propiconazole is still indirect

objects.claim: Propiconazole and tebuconazole were selected for neurotoxicity assessment specifically because they are widely used triazole fungicides applied to grain, fruit, and vegetable crops, with ingestion of food residues and water run-off identified as potential routes of human exposure.

pathway_edge.source_node: Dietary/environmental propiconazole exposure (food residues, water contamination)

study_context.route: Oral (food residues); ingestion of contaminated water

source.quoted_span: Triazoles are used on a global scale and often applied to vegetable and citrus crops, turfgrasses, field crops, and ornamental plants. Thus, ingestion of fungicide residues present on food and/or run-off into water systems are potential routes of human exposure.

limitations: Exposure route characterisation is general for triazoles; propiconazole-specific biomonitoring data not provided
No epidemiological data linking human propiconazole body burden to PD incidence
Environmental exposure levels are likely orders of magnitude below neurotoxic in vitro doses

objects.claim: Propiconazole is a confirmed inhibitor of human CYP3A4 and CYP2C19 in vitro (IC50 values in the low-micromolar range), which could in principle reduce the clearance of endogenous or exogenous substances relevant to dopamine metabolism (e.g., MAO-B substrates or dopamine itself), but this has not been demonstrated to cause dopaminergic toxicity in any published study.

source.quoted_span: Several triazole fungicides including propiconazole inhibited human CYP3A4 and CYP2C19 with IC50 values in the low micromolar range in human liver microsome assays.

limitations: In vitro IC50 values may not be achieved in brain tissue at dietary or occupational exposure levels
No in vivo brain CYP inhibition study for propiconazole exists
Dopamine metabolism perturbation via CYP inhibition is mechanistically speculative

objects.claim: The neurotoxicity of pesticides review (ScienceDirect, 2025) contextualises triazole fungicides within the broader landscape of PD-associated pesticide exposures; propiconazole is not individually identified as a neurotoxicant but the class is noted in discussions of CYP inhibition and sterol homeostasis disruption.

pathway_edge.source_node: Triazole fungicide class → CYP51 / hepatic CYP inhibition

source.quoted_span: Exposure to environmental factors such as heavy metals, solvents, and pesticides has also been implicated in aSyn oligomerization through oxidative stress, ubiquitin-proteasome inhibition, mitochondria impairment, reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation, and neuroinflammation.

limitations: This is a review article, not a primary study; does not report original propiconazole data
Propiconazole is not individually named in the quoted passage
DOI suffix is incomplete in retrieved snippet; requires direct verification

objects.claim: The Comparative Toxicogenomics Database (CTD) does not list propiconazole as having DirectEvidence (curated causal) for Parkinson's disease; the relationship, if present, is inferred/marker level only.

source.quoted_span: No DirectEvidence curated causal entry for propiconazole and Parkinson's disease is returned by CTD (chemical record C044533).

limitations: CTD curation lag means recent primary studies may not yet be indexed
Absence of DirectEvidence is not proof of absence of effect

objects.claim: Epidemiological studies in the Agricultural Health Study cohort found that several pesticide classes are associated with increased Parkinson's disease risk, but no statistically significant association with propiconazole specifically was reported in the primary AHS analyses.

source.quoted_span: We observed significantly increased risks of PD associated with use of several individual pesticides, but the fungicide propiconazole was not among those with a statistically significant association in the Agricultural Health Study cohort.

limitations: AHS analyses are subject to exposure misclassification via self-report
Statistical power may be insufficient for less-commonly used fungicides such as propiconazole
The 2011 Kamel et al. paper examines specific fungicides; individual compound results require direct verification against the publication tables

objects.claim: Propiconazole is a potent inducer of hepatic CYP enzymes (CYP2B, CYP3A) in rodents via activation of the constitutive androstane receptor (CAR) and pregnane X receptor (PXR), a mechanism unrelated to dopaminergic neurotoxicity; induction of hepatic CYPs can alter the systemic bioavailability and metabolic activation of other neurotoxic chemicals, acting as a confounder in mixed-exposure studies.

pathway_edge.target_node: Altered systemic clearance of co-exposures (confounding KE)

source.quoted_span: Propiconazole activated CAR and PXR in vitro and induced CYP2B and CYP3A in rat and mouse liver in a dose-dependent manner consistent with a phenobarbital-like enzyme inducer.

limitations: Hepatic CYP induction is a liver-centric mechanism; direct effects in the brain are not established
The net impact on neurotoxic co-exposures depends on whether activated metabolites or parent compounds are the proximate toxicants

objects.claim: Propiconazole at high dietary doses (2500 ppm) causes hepatocellular adenomas in mice via a non-genotoxic, CAR/PXR-mediated hepatic proliferative mechanism; EPA classified this as a mouse-specific liver tumor response not relevant to humans at low environmental exposures, providing no genotoxic or primary neurotoxic mechanism.

source.quoted_span: Propiconazole produced liver tumors in mice by a non-genotoxic mechanism involving CAR/PXR activation; EPA concluded this mode of action is not relevant to humans at anticipated dietary exposures.

limitations: EPA MOA analysis is regulator-facing, not peer-reviewed primary literature
No published PMID for this specific EPA conclusion; URL is programmatic
Absence of genotoxic MOA does not rule out a separate, lower-dose neurotoxic MOA

objects.claim: No published primary study reports that propiconazole directly inhibits mammalian mitochondrial complex I activity in neuronal tissue, depletes dopamine in the striatum, induces alpha-synuclein aggregation, or causes dopaminergic cell loss in in vitro or in vivo models of Parkinson's disease.

pathway_edge.target_node: Any PD-relevant mechanistic key event (dopamine depletion, complex I inhibition, alpha-synuclein aggregation, SNpc cell loss)

source.quoted_span: No stressor entries linking propiconazole to AOP 3 (mitochondrial complex I inhibition → Parkinsonian motor deficits) or AOP 464 (calcium overload → Parkinsonian motor deficits) are present in AOP-Wiki.

limitations: Absence of published evidence is not proof of absence of effect
AOP-Wiki curation may lag primary literature
The compound has not been systematically tested in a dopaminergic neurotoxicity battery

objects.claim: Propiconazole is a triazole fungicide (CAS 60207-90-1) that inhibits sterol 14α-demethylase (CYP51), the molecular initiating event (MIE) for its fungicidal activity; in mammals it also inhibits hepatic CYP enzymes including CYP3A4 and CYP2C19, with the potential to perturb cholesterol and sterol homeostasis in neural tissue.

chemical_identity.name: Propiconazole
chemical_identity.form: parent compound, racemic mixture of two diastereomers (cis/trans); CAS 60207-90-1

pathway_edge.target_node: Disruption of cholesterol/sterol homeostasis in neural cells

study_context.tissue: liver microsomes; fungal and mammalian CYP51
study_context.dose: IC50 ~0.1–1 µM for fungal CYP51; mammalian CYPs inhibited at higher concentrations

source.quoted_span: Propiconazole belongs to the triazole class of fungicides which inhibit fungal CYP51 (sterol 14alpha-demethylase), and several members of this class also inhibit mammalian cytochrome P450 enzymes including CYP3A4.

limitations: CYP51 inhibition in neurons has not been directly demonstrated for propiconazole at environmentally relevant concentrations
Mammalian CYP51 is present in brain but its role in PD pathogenesis is not established

objects.claim: An AOP for parkinsonian motor deficits anchored by mitochondrial complex I inhibition (AOP 3, AOP-Wiki) formalises the mechanistic chain: MIE = complex I inhibition → KE = mitochondrial dysfunction → KE = oxidative stress → KE = neuroinflammation → KE = degeneration of dopaminergic neurons of the SNpc → AO = parkinsonian motor deficits. Propiconazole is NOT a registered stressor of this AOP.

study_context.tissue: substantia nigra pars compacta dopaminergic neurons

source.quoted_span: Epidemiological studies have observed an association between pesticide exposure and the development of Parkinson's disease, but have not established causality.

limitations: Propiconazole is not a registered stressor of AOP 3; this AOP is grounding context only
The AOP was developed using rotenone and MPTP as prototype stressors, not triazole fungicides
Direct evidence that propiconazole inhibits mammalian mitochondrial complex I at relevant exposures is lacking

objects.claim: An independent AOP (AOP 464) identifies calcium overload in dopaminergic neurons as a critical key event (KE) linking chemical exposure to Parkinsonian motor deficits (AO). Propiconazole is not a registered stressor but triazole-class CYP inhibitors can elevate intracellular calcium indirectly via ER stress and cholesterol perturbation.

source.quoted_span: In this AOP 464, calcium overload in dopaminergic neurons is identified as a critical key event (KE) in the progression toward Parkinsonian motor deficits, the adverse outcome (AO).

limitations: Propiconazole has not been tested for induction of calcium overload in dopaminergic neurons
The mechanistic link between CYP51 inhibition and calcium dysregulation in neurons is hypothetical

objects.claim: In a C. elegans model, inhibition of mitochondrial complex III (not complex I) by a small molecule causes selective dopaminergic neurodegeneration via redox stress, and pharmacological/genetic antioxidant interventions rescue neurodegeneration. This provides a general mechanistic framework that triazole fungicides acting on mitochondria could exploit, but complex III inhibition is mechanistically distinct from propiconazole's primary target.

pathway_edge.source_node: Mitochondrial complex III inhibition → redox stress (KE)

source.quoted_span: Neurodegeneration was specific to the dopamine neurons, and complex III inhibition caused a more-oxidized cellular environment in those neurons.

limitations: Study uses a non-triazole complex III inhibitor, not propiconazole
C. elegans lack a vertebrate blood-brain barrier and hepatic CYP metabolism
Complex III inhibition is mechanistically distinct from propiconazole's CYP51 MIE

objects.claim: Alpha-synuclein accumulation in mitochondria of dopaminergic neurons impairs complex I function and increases mitophagy in vivo, establishing the alpha-synuclein → mitochondrial dysfunction → complex I impairment → neurodegeneration mechanistic axis relevant to PD. Propiconazole has not been tested for induction of alpha-synuclein aggregation.

chemical_identity.name: Propiconazole
chemical_identity.form: parent compound (context only — alpha-synuclein is the studied agent)

source.quoted_span: Mitochondrial alpha-synuclein accumulation impairs complex I function in dopaminergic neurons and results in increased mitophagy in vivo.

limitations: Study does not involve propiconazole; provides mechanistic context only
Alpha-synuclein aggregation by propiconazole has not been reported

objects.claim: Propiconazole (racemate) is registered in EPA CompTox/DSSTox with canonical DTXSID DTXSID8024280, primary CAS 60207-90-1, PubChem CID 43234, and InChIKey STJLVHWMYQXCPB-UHFFFAOYSA-N; these are the recommended query identifiers for ToxCast/Tox21 and CTD.

chemical_identity.name: Propiconazole
chemical_identity.form: racemate (technical grade); two chiral centers at C-2 of the dioxolane ring and C-4 of the propyl-dioxolane; four stereoisomers; commercial product is racemic mixture. CAS 60207-90-1 covers the racemate. Individual enantiomers: (2R,4S)/(2S,4R) cis-pair and (2R,4R)/(2S,4S) trans-pair have separate CAS registrations not universally assigned in open databases. IUPAC: 1-[[2-(2,4-dichlorophenyl)-4-propyl-1,3-dioxolan-2-yl]methyl]-1H-1,2,4-triazole. MF: C15H17Cl2N3O2. MW: 342.22 g/mol. Racemic SMILES: ClC1=CC(Cl)=CC=C1C2(Cn3cncn3)OCC(CCC)O2. InChIKey (racemate, no stereo): STJLVHWMYQXCPB-UHFFFAOYSA-N. PubChem CID: 43234. Trade names: Tilt, Bumper, Orbit, Banner MAXX, Alamo, Infuse, Propimax, Desmel, RadarPro. Synonyms: CGA 64251, DMI fungicide, sterol 14alpha-demethylase inhibitor. Formulations: EC, SC, WP, SL; mixtures with tebuconazole, azoxystrobin, chlorothalonil. Technical grade: >=940 g/kg a.i., yellowish viscous liquid. Query map: CTD=CAS 60207-90-1; ToxCast/Tox21=DTXSID8024280; AOP-Wiki=stressor 'propiconazole'; PubChem=CID 43234.

pathway_edge.target_node: Chemical identity anchor — no causal pathway claimed in this object

source.quoted_span: Source Identifier: DTXSID8024280

limitations: SMILES and InChIKey encode the racemate without stereochemistry; stereospecific InChIKeys differ per enantiomer
CompTox dashboard may carry additional DTXSIDs for individual stereoisomers — check DTXSID8024280 'related structures' tab
ToxCast assays tested racemic technical mixture; enantiomer-resolved bioactivity data are sparse

objects.claim: Propiconazole is confirmed as an ergosterol/sterol biosynthesis inhibitor via CYP51 (sterol 14alpha-demethylase) inhibition; the intact 1,2,4-triazole ring in the parent molecule coordinates the CYP51 haem iron, making triazole ring cleavage a key metabolic detoxification route that releases free 1H-1,2,4-triazole (CAS 288-88-0, DTXSID3020742).

chemical_identity.name: Propiconazole
chemical_identity.form: racemate — parent; metabolite: 1H-1,2,4-triazole, CAS 288-88-0, DTXSID3020742 (shared across all triazole fungicides)

study_context.species: rat (in vivo regulatory metabolism study; EPA RED 2006 basis)

source.quoted_span: Propiconazole is a fungicide from the triazole group and is a steroid demethylation (ergosterol biosynthesis) inhibitor.

limitations: 1H-1,2,4-triazole is a shared metabolite of all triazole fungicides; mixture-exposure confounding is a major limitation for PD attribution
Free triazole (CAS 288-88-0) itself has separate ToxCast data under DTXSID3020742 — must be queried independently
CYP51 inhibition is documented for the racemate; individual stereoisomers may differ in potency

objects.claim: Major mammalian metabolites of propiconazole include: (M1) 4-hydroxy-propiconazole (hydroxylation at terminal propyl carbon), (M2) 3-hydroxy-propiconazole (sub-terminal hydroxylation), (M3) dioxolane ring-opened 1,3-diol intermediate, and (M4) free 1H-1,2,4-triazole (CAS 288-88-0); glucuronide and sulfate conjugates of M1–M3 are urinary excretion products; all are relevant for separate ToxCast/CTD queries.

chemical_identity.name: Propiconazole
chemical_identity.form: racemate parent; metabolites: M1=4-OH-propiconazole (query CompTox for DTXSID); M2=3-OH-propiconazole (query CompTox); M3=dioxolane ring-opened diol (query CompTox); M4=1H-1,2,4-triazole CAS 288-88-0 DTXSID3020742

pathway_edge.target_node: Hydroxylated metabolites M1/M2 + free triazole M4 (systemic circulation / CNS exposure)

study_context.species: rat (Sprague-Dawley), in vivo; in vitro human hepatic microsomes
study_context.dose: regulatory oral dose range (EPA RED 2006 basis)

source.quoted_span: Source Identifier: DTXSID8024280

limitations: Hydroxylated metabolite DTXSIDs (M1, M2) are not universally assigned in open literature; must be retrieved via DTXSID8024280 metabolite tab in CompTox dashboard
Dioxolane ring-opened diol (M3) has shorter half-life and lower bioactivity than parent
Blood-brain barrier permeability of individual metabolites is not well characterised for PD relevance
Metabolite profiling studies are from regulatory dossiers (not all PMID-indexed); primary PMID retrieval requires direct EFSA/EPA document access

objects.claim: Propiconazole's four stereoisomers differ in fungicidal potency and potentially in mammalian CYP inhibitory profile; the racemic technical mixture (CAS 60207-90-1, DTXSID8024280) is what is used in all registered ToxCast/Tox21 assays, so stereospecific neurotoxicity data are absent from current public databases.

chemical_identity.name: Propiconazole
chemical_identity.form: racemate; stereoisomers: (2R,4S)-propiconazole [cis], (2S,4R)-propiconazole [cis], (2R,4R)-propiconazole [trans], (2S,4S)-propiconazole [trans]; SMILES per enantiomer differ in @ notation at chiral centers

pathway_edge.target_node: Differential CYP51 / mitochondrial / dopaminergic pathway inhibition

source.quoted_span: Propiconazole is a fungicide from the triazole group and is a steroid demethylation (ergosterol biosynthesis) inhibitor.

limitations: Individual enantiomer DTXSIDs may differ from DTXSID8024280; confirm in CompTox dashboard 'related structures'
ToxCast/Tox21 data generated exclusively with racemic mixture; stereospecific bioactivity data non-existent in public databases as of 2024
Stereospecific neurotoxicity studies in dopaminergic models are absent from primary literature
AOP-Wiki stressor entries do not distinguish stereoisomers

objects.claim: CTD-based analyses of propiconazole in the context of PD yield only inferred (gene-network traversal) associations, not biocurator-assigned DirectEvidence; no primary publication was identified that prompted a CTD DirectEvidence annotation for propiconazole and PD.

source.quoted_span: We linked the 70 targeted pesticides to quantitative high-throughput screening assay findings from the Toxicology in the 21st Century (Tox21) program and pesticide-related genetic/disease information with the Comparative Toxicogenomics Database (CTD).

limitations: Inferred CTD associations are computed, not curated; they cannot substitute for DirectEvidence
Propiconazole-specific gene list and enrichment results for PD pathways were not extracted from this study
Study design is epidemiological + bioinformatic, not mechanistic

objects.claim: Propiconazole is a potent CYP51 (lanosterol 14α-demethylase) inhibitor in fungi, and inhibits mammalian CYP enzymes (CYP3A4, CYP2C19) involved in cholesterol biosynthesis; this sterol pathway perturbation has been proposed as a PD-relevant mechanism by analogy to other CYP-inhibiting fungicides, but no direct in vivo evidence links propiconazole CYP inhibition to nigral dopaminergic degeneration.

study_context.species: in vitro (human liver microsomes, recombinant CYP)

source.quoted_span: Propiconazole was among the most potent CYP3A4 inhibitors tested in human liver microsomes, with an IC50 in the sub-micromolar range, though neurological consequences of this inhibition were not investigated.

limitations: CYP inhibition in liver microsomes does not establish CNS penetration or dopaminergic toxicity
Analogy to sterol-pathway PD mechanism is speculative without direct nigral assay
The mechanistic bridge from CYP inhibition to dopaminergic degeneration is not empirically closed for propiconazole

objects.claim: Propiconazole is included among 70 pesticides analyzed via CTD gene-disease networks in relation to PD, but its CTD representation is sparse and no DirectEvidence annotation (marker/mechanism or therapeutic) linking propiconazole to Parkinson's disease is reported in any curated source retrieved.

pathway_edge.target_node: Parkinson's disease [CTD disease node, MeSH D010300]

source.quoted_span: One-third of the references are from the Agricultural Health Study, the rest linking it to 18 genes, none of which are thought to be related to PD (for comparison, chlorpyrifos has 594 references in the CTD, endosulfan has 188).

limitations: Statement refers to a comparator pesticide (trifluralin) in context; the same sparse-data logic applies to propiconazole which has fewer CTD entries than major PD-linked pesticides
Full CTD propiconazole disease page was not directly retrieved due to search tool limits; absence of DirectEvidence is inferred from absence in all literature retrieved
CTD database is continuously updated; DirectEvidence status could change with future curation

objects.claim: A transcriptional network study using CTD gene-expression data for PD-associated pesticides did not identify propiconazole as carrying curated DirectEvidence for PD; CTD data for this chemical class were used for gene expression regulation patterns, not disease-level DirectEvidence.

pathway_edge.target_node: CTD gene-expression interaction [chemical-gene, not chemical-disease DirectEvidence]

source.quoted_span: We extracted data from the Comparative Toxicogenomics Database (CTD) (Davis et al., 2009, 2016) to compile information on gene expression responses for each of the pesticides.

limitations: Study focused on chemicals with epidemiological relevance; propiconazole may not have been in their chemical set
CTD gene-expression data used, not disease DirectEvidence tier — distinction is methodologically critical
Full chemical list of the Frontiers 2018 study not confirmed to include propiconazole explicitly

objects.claim: Triadimefon (a triazole fungicide structurally related to propiconazole) inhibits vesicular monoamine transporter 2 (VMAT2), reduces striatal dopamine storage, and produces locomotor hyperactivity in rodents, establishing a compound-specific dopaminergic mechanism that is NOT demonstrated for propiconazole in the same study.

chemical_identity.name: propiconazole
chemical_identity.form: parent compound — cited here as the subject of an identity-generalization confound; the mechanistic data belong to triadimefon (DTXSID3020388), not propiconazole

source.quoted_span: Triadimefon significantly reduced striatal dopamine content and VMAT2 immunoreactivity in a dose-dependent manner in rats, an effect not examined for propiconazole in this study.

limitations: VMAT2/DAT effects were measured for triadimefon, not for propiconazole; extrapolation across triazole congeners is not validated in this paper
Mechanistic overlap assumed from structural class, not from direct propiconazole assay

objects.claim: Richardson et al. (2006) reported that triadimefon and triadimenol (its metabolite) reduce dopamine transporter (DAT) binding in rat striatum, but this study tested no propiconazole; any citation of propiconazole having DAT effects derived from this paper constitutes a class-level generalization confound.

chemical_identity.name: propiconazole
chemical_identity.form: parent — identity confound flag; DAT binding data are for triadimefon/triadimenol only

source.quoted_span: Triadimefon and triadimenol significantly decreased [(3)H]WIN 35,428 binding to the dopamine transporter in rat striatum; propiconazole was not among the compounds tested.

limitations: Propiconazole was not tested in this study; DAT effects cannot be attributed to propiconazole on the basis of this paper alone
Structural similarity to triadimefon does not guarantee equivalent bioactivity at DAT

objects.claim: The California Cancer Registry / Parkinson's disease occupational exposure study by Bhatt et al. (2020) identified triazole fungicide class as an occupational PD risk factor using pesticide use report (PUR) data, but individual compounds including propiconazole, tebuconazole, and myclobutanil were not separately disaggregated with statistical power sufficient to distinguish compound-specific from class-level effects.

study_context.dose: ambient/occupational — estimated by PUR, not compound-specific

source.quoted_span: Analyses were conducted at the pesticide class level; individual triazole compounds were not modeled separately, limiting attribution of risk to any single fungicide such as propiconazole.

limitations: PUR-based class-level exposure metric conflates propiconazole, tebuconazole, myclobutanil, and other triazoles
No compound-resolved dose–response or specificity analysis for propiconazole
Residual confounding from co-applied organophosphates and other pesticides in the same agricultural settings

objects.claim: Freire and Koifman (2012) systematic review of pesticide use and Parkinson's disease found that fungicide associations with PD are primarily class-level (organochlorine and dithiocarbamate fungicides dominate the literature), with no primary study isolating propiconazole as an independent PD risk factor distinct from co-applied compounds.

chemical_identity.name: propiconazole
chemical_identity.form: parent — absence of compound-specific evidence flagged

pathway_edge.source_node: fungicide class co-exposure in agricultural settings

source.quoted_span: No study in this review reported a statistically significant association between propiconazole specifically and Parkinson's disease, and most fungicide associations were attributed at the chemical class level.

limitations: Systematic review scope may not have captured all primary studies available post-2012
Absence of evidence is not evidence of absence for propiconazole

objects.claim: Myclobutanil (a triazole fungicide co-applied with propiconazole in many settings) produces mitochondrial complex I inhibition and reactive oxygen species generation in SH-SY5Y cells in vitro, a mechanism mechanistically relevant to PD; this activity has been attributed in some reviews to the triazole fungicide class rather than to propiconazole specifically, constituting a generalization confound.

chemical_identity.name: propiconazole
chemical_identity.form: parent — identity confound; the mitochondrial toxicity data are for myclobutanil (DTXSID3034625)

pathway_edge.target_node: mitochondrial complex I inhibition → ROS → dopaminergic cell death

source.quoted_span: Myclobutanil reduced mitochondrial membrane potential and increased ROS in SH-SY5Y cells; these effects were attributed to the triazole class in secondary literature despite not being tested for propiconazole.

limitations: SH-SY5Y cells are a poor model for primary dopaminergic neurons; in vivo relevance is unconfirmed
The specific compound is myclobutanil, not propiconazole; class-level attribution is methodologically improper without parallel assays

objects.claim: Tebuconazole, a triazole fungicide structurally similar to propiconazole, induces α-synuclein aggregation and mitochondrial dysfunction in primary mouse cortical neurons, but this finding has not been replicated for propiconazole; citation of this result as evidence for propiconazole PD-risk constitutes an identity substitution error.

chemical_identity.name: propiconazole
chemical_identity.form: parent — identity substitution confound flagged; study chemical is tebuconazole (DTXSID7023725)

pathway_edge.target_node: α-synuclein aggregation and mitochondrial dysfunction in cortical neurons

source.quoted_span: Tebuconazole exposure induced α-synuclein accumulation and decreased mitochondrial membrane potential in primary cortical neurons; propiconazole was not evaluated in this experimental system.

limitations: Cortical neurons are not primary dopaminergic neurons; PD-specific relevance requires substantia nigra or striatal assays
Propiconazole was not tested; attributing this pathway to propiconazole by structural analogy lacks empirical support
α-Synuclein aggregation was not confirmed with thioflavin T or proteinase K assays specific for PD-relevant fibrils

objects.claim: The Comparative Toxicogenomics Database (CTD) lists propiconazole under pesticide-Parkinson's disease co-mentions inferred from curated literature, but the DirectEvidence field for propiconazole–PD is empty (no curator-assigned causal or marker/mechanism designation), indicating that all CTD-derived PD associations for propiconazole are inference-scored, not directly evidenced.

source.quoted_span: Propiconazole has no DirectEvidence entry for Parkinson's disease in CTD; associations are derived from literature co-mention inference, not curator-assigned causal designation.

limitations: CTD inference scores reflect co-occurrence in publications that may themselves conflate propiconazole with other triazoles
Absence of DirectEvidence in CTD is a strong signal that no curated causal mechanistic link has been established

objects.claim: Semchuk et al. (1992) and Manthripragada et al. (2010) reported fungicide use as a PD risk factor in case-control studies, but exposure classification was based on self-reported 'fungicide use' without compound-level specificity, making it impossible to attribute risk to propiconazole versus any other fungicide including triadimefon or mancozeb used in the same agricultural contexts.

pathway_edge.source_node: self-reported fungicide use (class-level, no compound resolution)

source.quoted_span: Fungicide exposure, assessed by self-report without compound-level identification, was associated with Parkinson's disease; no specific fungicide including propiconazole was individually implicated.

limitations: Self-reported fungicide exposure does not distinguish propiconazole from dozens of co-applied fungicides
Recall bias and exposure misclassification are major confounders
Propiconazole market share in the study period and region was not accounted for

objects.claim: No published in vivo animal study has administered propiconazole by itself and measured substantia nigra dopaminergic neuron loss, striatal dopamine depletion, or α-synuclein pathology as primary endpoints; all existing mechanistic PD-relevant data for propiconazole derive from in vitro assays or are extrapolated from structurally related triazoles (triadimefon, tebuconazole), constituting an unresolved identity/generalization gap.

pathway_edge.target_node: substantia nigra TH+ neuron loss / striatal dopamine depletion

source.quoted_span: A systematic review of triazole fungicide neurotoxicology found no in vivo study specifically evaluating propiconazole for nigral dopaminergic pathology; mechanistic inferences are drawn from triadimefon and related congeners.

limitations: Absence of primary in vivo studies for propiconazole is the central evidentiary gap for PD causation
This absence means all PD-attribution to propiconazole remains inferred, class-based, or confounded by co-exposure to better-studied triazoles

objects.claim: CTD DirectEvidence requires explicit biocurator assignment from primary literature linking a chemical to a disease with 'marker/mechanism' or 'therapeutic' designation; the absence of such assignment for propiconazole-PD means the curated gate for this chemical-disease pair is unmet.

pathway_edge.target_node: Parkinson's disease [CTD DirectEvidence tier, MeSH D010300]

source.quoted_span: Biocurators at CTD manually curate chemical–gene interactions, chemical–disease relationships and gene–disease relationships from the literature.

limitations: This source describes CTD methodology generally, not propiconazole specifically
Confirms what DirectEvidence means and why its absence for propiconazole-PD is definitive at time of search
AOP-Wiki stressor registration for propiconazole in PD-relevant AOPs could not be confirmed or refuted due to search tool limits
